title: Catherine

jokes.text: stupid fucking midget. / clearly he hasn't heard you sing.
jokes.setup: Selina calls columnist Mark Rivera a 'stupid fucking midget' for calling her a diva.
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately defends her by saying 'clearly he hasn't heard you sing' — implying she's also a diva in that sense, but presented as sincere flattery, making Gary look obliviously sycophantic.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gary moment — his defense accidentally confirms the 'diva' accusation. The double meaning (diva singer vs. diva personality) lands cleanly and is entirely character-specific. Only Gary would say this with total sincerity.

jokes.text: or should i say canine-didates?
jokes.setup: Gary is presenting dog candidates to Selina.
jokes.punchline: 'Canine-didates' — a forced pun that Gary offers proudly.
jokes.explanation: The pun itself is groan-worthy by design, but the real joke is Selina's instant 'no, you shouldn't say that' shutdown. Works as character comedy — Gary's eagerness to please paired with a terrible joke is very on-brand. The structure of offer/instant rejection is clean.

jokes.text: no, you shouldn't say that.
jokes.setup: Gary proudly offers the pun 'canine-didates.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat, immediate dismissal with zero hesitation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words do the full comedic work. Selina's authority and contempt are established instantly. The delivery is everything here; it's a reaction beat that defines the Selina/Gary dynamic.

jokes.text: a terrier is a diva dog. forget it. i don't need a ball of yappy hair shitting its body weight every 30 minutes.
jokes.setup: Gary shows Selina a terrier as a dog candidate.
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating rejection: 'diva dog' connects to the episode's diva theme, then the grotesque image of a dog 'shitting its body weight every 30 minutes.'
jokes.explanation: The 'diva dog' callback to the episode's running theme is clever, and the gross physical image is a great comedic escalation. Selina's specificity ('30 minutes') makes it funnier than a generic complaint.

jokes.text: i feel like a bad mom, you know? 'cause i never let catherine get a dog when she was little. this is a fresh start for us. it's parental ground zero.
jokes.setup: Selina explains why she wants a dog — as maternal reconnection.
jokes.punchline: 'Parental ground zero' — using military/disaster language for getting a dog reveals how she frames everything in grandiose political terms, and foreshadows how she'll immediately deprioritize Catherine all episode.
jokes.explanation: Gets funnier on rewatch knowing she abandons Catherine repeatedly. The grandiose framing of a dog purchase as 'parental ground zero' is very Selina. Subtle irony that pays off structurally across the episode.

jokes.text: simon? what an unbelievable name.
jokes.setup: Mike reveals his dog is named Simon, a completely normal dog name.
jokes.punchline: Selina treats it as remarkable — either genuine bafflement or performative charm.
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: Selina's oblivious VIP-bubble charm offensive, and the dramatic irony that Simon doesn't exist. Gets a rewatch bonus when we learn about the fake dog.

jokes.text: to drink or applied directly to the coat?
jokes.setup: Mike says he keeps his dog's coat shiny by keeping him hydrated — 'lots of water.'
jokes.punchline: Amy's withering follow-up question implies Mike is stupid enough that clarification is needed.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical, and character-appropriate for Amy's cutting intelligence. Also funnier in retrospect knowing Simon is fake — Mike is earnestly giving dog care tips for an imaginary animal. Perfect Veep-style dry wit.

jokes.text: leave my fake dog alone.
jokes.setup: Amy mocks Mike's dog care advice.
jokes.punchline: Mike says 'leave my fake dog alone' — which lands as a joke in the moment as self-deprecating humor, but is accidentally literally true (Simon is fake), which the audience will discover later.
jokes.explanation: This is the sleeper joke of the episode. On first watch it reads as self-deprecating banter. On rewatch — or when the reveal lands later — it's a perfect piece of foreshadowing that doubles as a confession hiding in plain sight. The word 'fake' is doing enormous structural work. Justifies the 8+ score: it rewards the audience twice, once live and once retroactively.

jokes.text: maybe we should get a rescue dog 'cause it'll play great. as long as it's not one of those animals with three legs and a wheel. four legs good; three legs bad.
jokes.setup: Selina suggests a rescue dog for good optics.
jokes.punchline: She immediately qualifies it — no disabled dogs — then someone riffs 'four legs good, three legs bad' (Orwell reference), exposing the cynical calculation beneath the compassionate PR move.
jokes.explanation: The Orwell 'Animal Farm' riff ('four legs good, two legs bad') is clever and earns its place — using a totalitarian propaganda slogan to describe a VP's dog politics is sharp political satire. Selina's exclusion of disabled animals while claiming to be compassionate is great character work.

jokes.text: i'm sure he wouldn't miss it for the world if it weren't for the fact that he runs the world.
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers the news that the President won't attend Selina's 20th anniversary party.
jokes.punchline: His over-diplomatic spin: 'if it weren't for the fact that he runs the world' — patronizing and tone-deaf simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah delivery — obliviously condescending. The grandiose framing ('runs the world') while delivering a petty snub is very on-brand. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: i'm sure his absence has nothing to do with your rift with the first lady.
jokes.setup: Jonah, trying to be diplomatic, volunteers the exact sensitive information he should be avoiding.
jokes.punchline: By saying the rift has 'nothing to do' with it, he explicitly makes it about the rift.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's essential character trait — saying the wrong thing while trying to say the right thing. Well-executed but not exceptional.

jokes.text: she'd be a rough fighter, though. she's got big shoulders. those aren't pads.
jokes.setup: Selina insists there's no catfight with the First Lady.
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts herself by physically sizing up the First Lady as an opponent — 'those aren't pads.'
jokes.explanation: The setup/subversion is airtight: 'there's no catfight' immediately followed by tactical assessment of her rival's physical advantages. 'Those aren't pads' is specific, catty, and perfectly Selina. The self-contradiction is the joke and it's earned entirely through character.

jokes.text: be careful your ears don't pop on the way down.
jokes.setup: Jonah offers to 'keep his ear to the ground' about rift rumors.
jokes.punchline: Selina: 'Be careful your ears don't pop on the way down' — a devastatingly elegant insult implying he's very tall and stooping very low.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. It builds on a cliché ('ear to the ground'), twists it into a height joke (Jonah is famously tall), adds a physical comedy layer (altitude pressure), and demolishes him in 11 words. Perfect economy, structure, and character integration. Justifies 8+: it's the kind of insult that would be quoted in a writers' room as a gold standard of the form.

jokes.text: i was gonna say eva peron. yeah, i prefer that comparison. less of a mustache.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests removing giant photos of Selina so she doesn't look like 'Stalin.'
jokes.punchline: Amy corrects to Eva Perón; Selina prefers it — 'less of a mustache.'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'we shouldn't look like dictators' to debating which dictator is more flattering is sharp political satire. The mustache line is a clean, quick payoff. Well-structured two-beat joke.

jokes.text: does it get used in a clumsy and unpleasant analogy by you?
jokes.setup: Selina is extending a 'hoover dam filled with shit' analogy about what happens when they announce Chuck.
jokes.punchline: Amy pre-empts the analogy by naming the genre of what Selina is doing — 'a clumsy and unpleasant analogy by you.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional meta-joke. Amy critiques the rhetoric in real time, and the self-referential quality ('clumsy and unpleasant analogy by you') is both a character beat AND a writers' room joke about Selina's verbal style. The specificity of 'by you' makes it a personal takedown rather than general observation. Justifies 8+: it's doing three things simultaneously — character comedy, meta-commentary, and a sharp Amy insult.

jokes.text: i'm like the last guy in a human centipede with this.
jokes.setup: Selina explains she has to absorb everyone's political displeasure simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: She compares her position to the last person in a Human Centipede — getting all the shit, literally.
jokes.explanation: Audacious, shocking, and perfectly calibrated. The Human Centipede reference in a political context is deeply wrong in the best way. 'And there it is' from a staffer is the perfect reaction beat. Highly quotable and very Veep in its willingness to go somewhere truly vile for a metaphor. The image is viscerally accurate as political satire.

jokes.text: and there it is. [reaction beat to Human Centipede line]
jokes.setup: Selina drops the Human Centipede analogy.
jokes.punchline: Dan's three-word, resigned 'and there it is' — acknowledging the line was crossed without further comment.
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat amplifies the Human Centipede joke. 'And there it is' captures the weary normalization of Selina's extremity perfectly. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: he was rapey reeves. when was that guy ever respectful?
jokes.setup: Someone suggests it might be disrespectful to announce Chuck during Senator Reeves' memorial dedication.
jokes.punchline: Selina reveals the dead senator had a nickname: 'Rapey Reeves' — immediately undercutting any call for respect.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire — the revelation that the senator being memorialized was nicknamed 'Rapey Reeves' pulls the rug from under any solemn framing. Very dark, very Veep. The rhetorical logic ('when was that guy ever respectful?') is airtight.

jokes.text: does that exist, hurricane selina? i'm binging it.
jokes.setup: Someone calls the chaotic day 'Hurricane Selina.'
jokes.punchline: Selina takes it completely literally and asks someone to check whether Hurricane Selina is a real storm — 'I'm binging it' — turning a metaphor into a political crisis.
jokes.explanation: Strong setup for the hurricane running gag. Selina's narcissistic paranoia makes this entirely believable — of course she'd treat a hurricane named after her as a personal PR crisis. 'I'm binging it' as political-speak is a nice touch.

jokes.text: selina is on next year's list of hurricanes. what if it hits and we get headlines saying 'selina causing large-scale devastation'?
jokes.setup: The team discovers Selina is indeed the name of an upcoming hurricane.
jokes.punchline: The logical extrapolation — headlines of 'Selina causing large-scale devastation' — is both absurd and, for a VP, plausibly catastrophic optics.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the hurricane bit. The specificity of 'large-scale devastation' as a headline is funny. Works as satirical commentary on how political image-management extends to weather.

jokes.text: really, amy? 'cause i've met some people, okay? real people, and i've got to tell you, a lot of them are fucking idiots.
jokes.setup: Amy assures Selina that 'people won't equate you with a natural disaster.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'reassurance' that she's actually right to worry — because she's met the public and they're 'fucking idiots.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. Selina's contempt for voters, delivered as a sincere concern, is the thesis of the whole show compressed into two sentences. The beat 'real people' followed by 'fucking idiots' is perfectly structured. Justifies 8+: this is purely Selina, could only be her, and crystallizes the show's central satirical stance on politicians.

jokes.text: did you know old grandpa fumblepants couldn't even swim? he would just hang out in the shallow end and stare.
jokes.setup: At Senator Reeves' memorial, named after a recreation center, mourners are paying respects.
jokes.punchline: Selina, at a memorial, casually mocks the dead senator for being bad at swimming — 'grandpa fumblepants' — while surrounded by grieving people.
jokes.explanation: 'Grandpa fumblepants' is a vivid, funny nickname. The context (memorial event) makes the disrespect funnier. The image of him staring in the shallow end is oddly specific and creepy given his 'Rapey Reeves' nickname.

jokes.text: you know what his favorite stroke was? was it dick stroke? it was dick stroke.
jokes.setup: Selina is mocking the late 'Rapey Reeves' at his own memorial.
jokes.punchline: A swimming stroke called 'dick stroke' — the pun lands because of the 'Rapey Reeves' context established earlier.
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'Rapey Reeves' makes this land harder. The staff's complicity in the joke ('it was dick stroke') is a nice group cringe moment. Dark and earned given prior context.

jokes.text: f.y.i., ma'am, the president is not calling. / f.y.i., gary, no shit.
jokes.setup: Selina uses a fake presidential phone call to escape an awkward conversation about the rift with the First Lady.
jokes.punchline: Gary helpfully informs her it's fake; she icily confirms she knows.
jokes.explanation: Gary's inability to read a room — she's the one performing the fake call — is perfectly observed. 'No shit' at Secret Service discretion levels is very Selina. Clean two-beat structure.

jokes.text: you are like a... earlobe — you're just there, just wobbling.
jokes.setup: Amy is furious at Mike for stalling on announcing Chuck.
jokes.punchline: She searches for an insult and lands on 'earlobe' — a body part that is completely useless and just... there, wobbling. The hesitation before landing on 'earlobe' is part of the joke.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original insult. 'Earlobe' as a metaphor for useless passivity is unexpected, specific, and perfect. The descriptive extension — 'just there, just wobbling' — completes the image beautifully. Justifies 8+: it's the kind of insult that sounds improvised but is precisely constructed, capturing Amy's intelligence and frustration simultaneously.

jokes.text: i imagine that i would mix ape shit with bat shit, raise it to a whole new level of fury, then i'd probably rip your face off and use your eye sockets as a sex toy.
jokes.setup: Mike poses a 'hypothetical' about putting Chuck Furnham on the clean jobs task force.
jokes.punchline: Doyle's 'hypothetical' response escalates from 'ape shit' to 'bat shit' to facial removal to sexual assault with a skull — delivered calmly over canapés.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is flawless: the hypothetical framing gives Doyle permission to escalate to unhinged violence, and each beat ('ape shit,' 'bat shit,' 'whole new level of fury,' 'rip your face off,' 'eye sockets as a sex toy') tops the previous one. The delivery context — polite political event, canapés nearby, strangers passing — makes it devastating. The escalation taxonomy is perfectly executed. Justifies 8+: this is the kind of monologue that would be quoted for years.

jokes.text: it's just a hypothetical like yours.
jokes.setup: Doyle has just threatened to use Mike's eye sockets as a sex toy.
jokes.punchline: Mike's response: 'it's just a hypothetical like yours' — the calm, procedural comeback after an unhinged threat.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the Doyle threat sequence. Mike's bland procedural response to graphic threats is very Veep — the show's entire premise is bureaucratic language applied to insane situations. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: chuck's oily, but he's not evil oily. he's ex-oil. / noa hollow horse. that's a trojan horse. / he'd be a hollow trojan horse, sir.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to explain Chuck as a 'hollow horse' while arguing he's not really from oil.
jokes.punchline: Doyle corrects the metaphor ('that's a Trojan horse, not a hollow horse'); Dan pivots to 'hollow Trojan horse' — a self-undermining compromise that exposes the spin.
jokes.explanation: The mythological metaphor correction is sharp — Dan's confusion exposes that he's spinning without a clear argument. 'Hollow Trojan horse' as a capitulation is good. Also establishes 'oily but not evil oily' as a nice bit of political euphemism satire.

jokes.text: listen, while you take your fake phone call, is it okay if i steal dan for a second? or should i say steal dan back?
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-fake presidential phone call. Hallowes asks to 'steal' Dan.
jokes.punchline: 'Or should I say steal Dan back?' — implying Dan was poached from Hallowes and she knows about it. Also casually acknowledges the fake phone call.
jokes.explanation: Double-layered: she knows about the fake call (effortlessly owning Selina) and reveals Dan's history. The 'or should I say steal Dan back' restructure is elegantly done. Hallowes is established as a formidable rival in one line.

jokes.text: you know who's a fucking diva? that's a fucking diva. / that one.
jokes.setup: Selina has just been publicly outmaneuvered by Hallowes stealing Dan while Selina is on a fake phone call.
jokes.punchline: Selina calls Hallowes a diva — the exact accusation she's been fighting all episode — without any self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: Strong callback. The episode opens with Selina furious at being called a diva; she immediately uses the same word as her worst insult. The self-awareness she conspicuously lacks is the joke. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: you know, except for the whole secret service detail, i think i'm actually starting to blend in.
jokes.setup: Catherine talks about college life.
jokes.punchline: She thinks she's blending in despite having a Secret Service detail — the ultimate obstacle to blending in.
jokes.explanation: Solid observational irony. Catherine's obliviousness establishes her character efficiently. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: you dumped my daughter by text and didn't even apologize. / oh, no, i did. i signed off with colon open brackets.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Gary for texting Catherine a breakup (on Gary's behalf, presumably).
jokes.punchline: Gary's defense: he DID apologize — with an emoticon. Colon-open-brackets = ':(' — a frown face.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The specificity of 'colon open brackets' (the textual description of ':(' rather than just saying 'frowny face') is pitch-perfect Gary — technical, sincere, and completely tone-deaf. The structure is clean and the punchline is well-earned. Justifies 8+: it tells us everything about Gary's emotional intelligence in one absurd detail.

jokes.text: do you know what these oil wise guys call him? they call him 'who the fuck is chuck furnham?'
jokes.setup: Dan probes Hallowes about Chuck Furnham's standing with oil interests.
jokes.punchline: His entire reputation in oil circles is that nobody knows who he is — 'who the fuck is Chuck Furnham?' as a collective nickname.
jokes.explanation: The joke that a man's nickname in his own industry is a question expressing total ignorance of his existence is beautifully constructed. Makes the episode's entire Chuck subplot funny retroactively. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: i have some vice presidential m&ms that i would love for them to have. you see the seal right there? except we'll need another one because there are two grandchildren. / i'm out. / you're out? / i'm out.
jokes.setup: Selina is buttering up Chuck with branded VP M&Ms, then discovers she's run out.
jokes.punchline: The elaborate M&M presentation collapses when she reveals she only has one bag for two grandchildren — 'I'm out.' The VP has run out of her own branded merchandise in the middle of using it as a political bribe.
jokes.explanation: The VP M&M detail is excellent Veep world-building, and the humiliation of running out mid-presentation is perfectly cringe. 'I'm out' repeated flatly is very funny. Shows Selina's grandiose charm offensive undermined by logistics.

jokes.text: your husband was a massive player. politically speaking, of course.
jokes.setup: Jonah is consoling the widow of Senator Reeves at his memorial — 'Rapey Reeves.'
jokes.punchline: He calls the dead senator a 'massive player' (true in the worst way given the nickname), then adds 'politically speaking, of course' — which makes it worse, not better.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe joke. The callback to 'Rapey Reeves' makes 'massive player' land with full impact. 'Politically speaking, of course' is the perfect addition — it draws more attention to the double meaning rather than defusing it. Justifies 8+: the joke works on three levels (Jonah's obliviousness, the nickname callback, and the self-defeating qualifier).

jokes.text: i guess all those tears must be dehydrating. / i don't think it works like that. / you know, i'll just get the water, okay?
jokes.setup: Jonah is asked to get water for the grieving widow.
jokes.punchline: He ruminates on whether crying causes dehydration — to a grieving widow, at a memorial — then someone corrects him, then he retreats.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's tactlessness at a memorial is consistently funny. The quasi-scientific musing about tear dehydration to a widow is deliciously tone-deaf.

jokes.text: cock-blocking widow.
jokes.setup: After Jonah's interaction with the widow interrupts his ability to network.
jokes.punchline: His internal (or muttered) characterization of the grieving widow: 'cock-blocking widow.'
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly define Jonah's character. Maximum economy, maximum wrongness. Calling a grieving widow a 'cock-blocking widow' is outrageously Jonah — self-centered, crass, and completely lacking in social awareness. Justifies 8+: it's one of the most efficient character jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: is there even a name for that? / how about catch-44? / gary.
jokes.setup: Someone notes they have 'two catch-22 situations simultaneously' and wonders if there's a name for that.
jokes.punchline: Gary eagerly supplies 'catch-44' — a mathematically obvious answer that still somehow lands as a joke because of Gary's eager-to-please delivery, followed by Amy's disgusted 'Gary.'
jokes.explanation: The joke is really Amy's contemptuous 'Gary' — a single word doing the work of an entire reaction. Gary's mathematical literalism is a good character beat. The rhythm is tight.

jokes.text: it's really nice that my mom's getting a new dog. i always wanted a dog. / hey, catherine, you want some more coffee? / no, i'm still drinking this one.
jokes.setup: Catherine is left alone with junior staff and tries to make conversation about the dog she always wanted but never got.
jokes.punchline: Staff offer her more coffee to deflect; she points out she's still drinking the one she has. The awkward deflection and literal response captures her social isolation perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Understated cringe. Catherine's sincere comment about always wanting a dog (which Selina is now getting as a PR stunt) is quietly sad, and the staff's deflection is awkward. Functional character work.

jokes.text: you know, you don't have to entertain me. i'm cool with my own thoughts. / oh, thank god.
jokes.setup: Catherine, left with junior staff, graciously tells them they don't need to entertain her.
jokes.punchline: Staff member replies 'oh, thank god' — relieved rather than polite.
jokes.explanation: Clean two-beat joke. The rudeness of 'oh, thank god' in response to a VIP's daughter's graciousness is funny and characterizes the office culture. Good economy.

jokes.text: if you say chuck furnham, i will go into anaphylactic fucking shock. / i'm saying chuck furnham. / fucking hell!
jokes.setup: Sidney warns Amy in the strongest possible terms not to say Chuck Furnham's name.
jokes.punchline: Amy says it anyway; Sidney's reaction fulfills the threatened collapse — 'fucking hell!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff structure. The warning is so extreme that delivering the name becomes a comedic inevitability. 'Anaphylactic fucking shock' is a great choice of extreme reaction, and 'fucking hell!' as the actual response is funny in its relative restraint. Very well-constructed.

jokes.text: he's not oil. he's a fucking fossil, but he's not oil. / we need somebody who is plugged in. the only thing that chuck is plugged into is his fucking piss bag.
jokes.setup: Amy defends Chuck as 'not oil.'
jokes.punchline: Sidney's devastating reframe: not oil, but 'a fucking fossil' — then the escalation to the piss bag.
jokes.explanation: Two-stage joke that earns its place. 'Fossil' as a redirect from 'oil' is a sharp wordplay/insult hybrid (fossil fuels). Then 'the only thing he's plugged into is his fucking piss bag' takes it to an unexpected physical place. Both halves are strong; combined they're excellent. Justifies 8+: the 'fossil' pivot is genuinely clever and the piss bag escalation is committed and specific.

jokes.text: okay, where'd he go? / he must be in the bathroom. / that prostate of his. / get in there, mike. go. / this in no way will be demeaning.
jokes.setup: The team needs to keep Chuck contained, but he's disappeared to the bathroom.
jokes.punchline: The implication is prostate issues (old man); Mike is dispatched to follow him into the bathroom; Selina adds 'this in no way will be demeaning' — the understatement of the episode.
jokes.explanation: 'This in no way will be demeaning' as a Selina sign-off for sending Mike to surveil an old man in a bathroom is perfectly timed. The prostate detail is exactly specific enough to land.

jokes.text: widow walk. / people don't shout at you when you're standing next to the dead man's grieving widow.
jokes.setup: The team needs to avoid angry senators (Doyle, Hallowes, Sidney) closing in from multiple angles.
jokes.punchline: Dan's tactical solution: use the grieving widow as a human shield — 'people don't shout at you when you're standing next to the dead man's grieving widow.'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire compressed into a tactical move. 'Widow Walk' as a named strategy, and the clinical explanation of its mechanics — 'the dead man's grieving widow' as a grammatically specific shield — is exactly the kind of moral bankruptcy the show specializes in. Justifies 8+: it's a new political tactic named and explained in one line, and it's immediately executed.

jokes.text: now is it true that the first lady calls you creepy veepy?
jokes.setup: Mrs. Reeves invites Selina to 'juicy gossip' as they exit together — Selina thinks she's safe using the widow as a shield.
jokes.punchline: The widow's idea of 'juicy gossip' is the most damaging possible question — confirming the 'creepy veepy' nickname from the First Lady.
jokes.explanation: The widow walk strategy immediately backfires in the most specific way possible. 'Creepy veepy' is a great nickname, and the structure — Selina uses the widow for protection, widow immediately destabilizes her — is beautiful irony. Justifies 8+: the punchline ('creepy veepy') is quotable and the structural irony of the widow walk turning on her is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: i'm very excited about it. good-bye. / what are you excited about, chuck? are you the new face of louis vuitton?
jokes.setup: Chuck Furnham overhears Selina's fake enthusiasm ('I'm very excited about it') intended to deflect the widow's gossip question.
jokes.punchline: Chuck thinks it refers to him; Amy's withering 'are you the new face of Louis Vuitton?' mocks the idea of Chuck being exciting.
jokes.explanation: Good comic misunderstanding. 'Louis Vuitton' as the benchmark of exciting things nobody could confuse Chuck with is funny. The scene chaos is well-managed.

jokes.text: i as good as announced is not announced. you are as good as dead, but you're not actually dead... not yet.
jokes.setup: Chuck protests that Selina 'as good as announced' his appointment.
jokes.punchline: Selina's analogy: 'as good as dead' — pointing out that 'as good as' is not the same as reality, with the addition 'not yet' making it a veiled threat.
jokes.explanation: Elegant logical construction with a dark twist. The parallel structure ('as good as announced / as good as dead') is beautifully built, and 'not yet' turns it from explanation to threat. Selina's rhetorical skills are shown at their best — ruthless, precise, funny. Justifies 8+: this is the kind of line that shows the writers at full craft.

jokes.text: tell her that i'm cancelling the lunch that was supposed to prove there's nothing more important than catherine because something more important than catherine has come up.
jokes.setup: Selina realizes she's missed lunch with Catherine and asks Gary to call Sue to cancel.
jokes.punchline: Her message is a complete self-own — she literally explains that the lunch designed to show Catherine is her priority is being cancelled because Catherine isn't her priority.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode, possibly the series. Selina's message is a perfect Iannucci construction: it's simultaneously an instruction to Gary, a confession of her parenting failures, and a self-defeating tautology. The structure ('cancelled the lunch to prove there's nothing more important... because something more important came up') is logically airtight and devastating. Justifies 8+: this is Selina's character perfectly encapsulated and it's genuinely brilliantly written.

jokes.text: that's okay, gary. i got all that.
jokes.setup: Gary begins to relay Selina's complex self-incriminating message to Sue.
jokes.punchline: Sue has already heard the whole thing through the open line — 'I got all that.'
jokes.explanation: The button on the previous joke. Sue's flat, omniscient competence is her character in one line. 'I got all that' implies she heard everything including Selina's self-own. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: you guys ditched me at a pervert's memorial. that's some cold shit.
jokes.setup: Dan returns after being pulled away by Hallowes.
jokes.punchline: His complaint that he was 'ditched at a pervert's memorial' nonchalantly combines the political crisis and the Rapey Reeves running gag.
jokes.explanation: Good callback. 'Pervert's memorial' as casual shorthand for the event is very Veep — the show's world where sexual misconduct is just a background detail. 'That's some cold shit' as Dan's moral complaint about being left there (rather than about attending) is nice character irony.

jokes.text: come here. come, come. catherine, this is dan. he works in yo mama's office.
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Dan to Catherine while trying to hand her off.
jokes.punchline: 'He works in yo mama's office' — Selina using slang in an attempt to seem relatable to her daughter, which lands as deeply awkward.
jokes.explanation: Selina using 'yo mama' ironically/sincerely while being the mama in question is a good self-own. Shows her desperate awkwardness with Catherine through performance of relatability. The 'come, come' urgency before the forced casual is well-timed.

jokes.text: so why don't you tell dan about the-- you know, the... experimental theater course? / uh, sure. yeah. you can talk to him about that. you can talk about anything. / anything. / talk, talk, talk. / now that we have the permission to talk about anything... / have you ever read faulkner before?
jokes.setup: Selina desperately tries to broker a conversation between Dan and Catherine before abandoning Catherine yet again.
jokes.punchline: After multiple 'anything'-permissions from Selina, Catherine and Dan's actual conversation opener is 'have you ever read Faulkner?' — the most stilted possible use of 'anything.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating 'anything' permissions followed by the most awkward possible icebreaker is good sustained cringe. The pause before 'have you ever read Faulkner' is doing a lot of work. Shows Catherine's social difficulty and Dan's utter inability to engage with anything non-political.

jokes.text: a rubik's cube is not impossible to solve. i saw a chinese kid do it in like 10 seconds. 10 seconds, mike.
jokes.setup: Mike says the Chuck situation is 'like a fucking rubik's cube — impossible.'
jokes.punchline: Selina takes the metaphor literally and corrects it, citing a specific Chinese kid doing it in 10 seconds — dismissing Mike's hyperbole while revealing her own bizarre literal-mindedness.
jokes.explanation: '10 seconds, mike' as a mic-drop correction of a metaphor is good Selina. She processes the world in such a specific, defensive way that metaphors become literal challenges. Solid character joke.

jokes.text: yeah, it looks astonished. like it's attached to jumper cables or something.
jokes.setup: Catherine shows Dan the dog she's selected — presumably a bug-eyed or alert-looking breed.
jokes.punchline: Dan's description: the dog looks 'astonished,' like it's 'attached to jumper cables.'
jokes.explanation: 'Attached to jumper cables' as a description of a perpetually startled dog is specific and visual. Good observational comedy with a nice image.

jokes.text: what is that skirt? is it a rug?
jokes.setup: Selina pulls Amy aside to ask about Catherine privately, but first registers Catherine's outfit.
jokes.punchline: Her first comment about her daughter is a fashion critique — 'is it a rug?' — before asking how Catherine is doing.
jokes.explanation: Selina's priorities perfectly exposed — even in a private aside about her daughter's wellbeing, she leads with fashion. Very character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: we announce the dog. it's soft wash, breakfast news, but we tease it out. we say we're looking for a name. an ocean of ideas comes pouring in. meantime, we're buying time to figure all the other shit out. / that's kind of a great idea.
jokes.setup: The team is stuck on two impossible political problems simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: Selina's proposed solution to a major political crisis is: announce the dog and crowdsource a name to distract the media. And it's a genuinely good idea — which makes it sadder.
jokes.explanation: Good satirical joke about political media management — a dog announcement as a crisis deflection strategy. 'That's kind of a great idea' from Amy is a reluctant admission that makes the satire land harder.

jokes.text: ma'am, senator doyle just arrived and he has a face like he's been stabbed in the groin.
jokes.setup: Gary alerts Selina to Senator Doyle's arrival.
jokes.punchline: 'A face like he's been stabbed in the groin' — Gary's vivid, specific, slightly wrong idiom for Doyle's fury.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Gary line. The image is specific, physical, and slightly off — 'stabbed in the groin' is neither the face you'd make nor the place you'd expect, but it's precisely the look of furious wounded dignity that Doyle has. Gary's way of seeing the world through physical metaphor is perfectly his. Justifies 8+: it's an original, highly visual description that's become quotable.

jokes.text: wait, mom, what's wrong with your face? / nothing, darling. i'm pretending to talk to you. / but we are talking.
jokes.setup: Selina is using Catherine as cover to avoid Doyle, putting on a performed 'serious conversation' face.
jokes.punchline: Catherine notices the fake expression; Selina explains she's 'pretending to talk' to her; Catherine points out they ARE talking.
jokes.explanation: Perfect mother-daughter cringe. Selina performing a fake conversation with her actual daughter, who calls out both the fake face and the logical impossibility. 'But we are talking' is Catherine's character in a sentence — earnest, literal, slightly heartbreaking. Justifies 8+: the joke works on a comedic and an emotional level simultaneously.

jokes.text: 20 years ago, you had no power, but you had balls. / now look at you. / i have a dick and balls, okay?
jokes.setup: Doyle confronts Selina about her compromised political spine on the Chuck issue.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to 'you had balls' — 'I have a dick AND balls, okay?' — a power assertion that's also anatomically absurd coming from a woman, which is exactly the point.
jokes.explanation: Strong feminist-inflected power joke. Selina reclaims the gendered 'balls' metaphor by going further ('dick AND balls') in a way that's simultaneously assertive, absurd, and completely in character. The escalation from 'balls' to 'dick and balls' in one breath is perfectly timed. Justifies 8+: it's quotable, character-specific, and does real thematic work about gender in politics.

jokes.text: no, please. don't stop because of me. this is really interesting.
jokes.setup: Selina and Doyle are having a heated exchange about her political 'balls' when they notice Catherine.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's deadpan: 'don't stop because of me. this is really interesting' — a teenager watching her mother discuss her metaphorical genitalia with a senator.
jokes.explanation: Catherine's flat observational deadpan is a great character beat. Her detached amusement ('this is really interesting') at the absurdity she's grown up around is funny and slightly sad.

jokes.text: and that's not just the high notes. / i have a recital coming up. / okay. / you could have said 'don't blow it.' / no, i'm not gonna say that.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces a trumpet player to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina specifically refuses the easy 'don't blow it' trumpet pun — which Gary points out she could have made — demonstrating her (occasionally) functional awareness of terrible jokes.
jokes.explanation: Selina declining the obvious pun is a meta joke about comedic restraint. 'Not gonna say that' as a power move over a terrible joke is funny. The 'high notes' setup is nicely laid in.

jokes.text: look at gary go. he's like a human teleprompter for small talk. / he calls this gary-oke. / it's so weird. it's like he's the horse whisperer or something. not that i think your mother's a horse.
jokes.setup: Catherine and Dan watch Gary feed Selina biographical details about guests for small talk.
jokes.punchline: 'Gary-oke' is a great coinage; the 'horse whisperer' comparison followed by immediate apology ('not that I think your mother's a horse') is good cringe comedy.
jokes.explanation: 'Gary-oke' is genuinely clever — it captures Gary's function perfectly (he's the karaoke machine feeding Selina her lines). The horse whisperer extended apology is a nice character moment for Dan, showing his rare social awkwardness around someone he can't charm. Both jokes work well.

jokes.text: wife, not his daughter. wife, not daughter.
jokes.setup: Gary is feeding Selina info about Jim Wiseman and his companion.
jokes.punchline: Gary's urgent preemptive clarification — 'wife, not his daughter' — suggests the age gap is such that Selina might make a horrifying mistake.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — 'wife, not his daughter' implies an enormous amount about Jim Wiseman and his companion in five words. The repetition ('wife, not daughter') suggests Gary knows Selina might still get it wrong. Excellent visual gag inferred from dialogue.

jokes.text: mom's smile is starting to crack. it's like her divorce face.
jokes.setup: Catherine watches Selina work the room, her smile increasingly strained.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's clinical observation: 'it's like her divorce face' — implying she's seen this particular performance of strain before.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke from Catherine. 'Divorce face' is a perfectly specific coinage that tells us everything about Catherine's childhood — she watched her mother perform this same strained smile through the dissolution of her marriage. Dark and funny simultaneously. Justifies 8+: it does real characterization work in one detail.

jokes.text: i'm jonah, by the way. i work at the west wing of the white house. / as opposed to what, the west wing of graceland?
jokes.setup: Jonah introduces himself with his standard 'I work at the West Wing of the White House.'
jokes.punchline: Catherine's deadpan: 'as opposed to what, the West Wing of Graceland?' — deflating Jonah's self-importance instantly.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Catherine/Jonah moment. 'The West Wing of Graceland' is a perfect specific absurd alternative. The joke perfectly deflates Jonah's pomposity while establishing Catherine as sharper than anyone gives her credit for. Justifies 8+: the specificity of Graceland (not any building, but Graceland) and the economy of delivery make this one of the best individual lines of the episode.

jokes.text: it's kind of an open secret in washington that mike has an imaginary dog. / why? / gets him out of staying late for work. / we call it his bullshit-tzu.
jokes.setup: The dog announcement has prompted discussion of Mike's dog, Simon.
jokes.punchline: Dan reveals Simon is a fictional dog used to escape work; nickname: 'bullshit-tzu' (bullshit + shih tzu).
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's longest-running setup. 'Bullshit-tzu' is a genuinely brilliant portmanteau — it's the perfect fake dog breed name, the fake dog gets a fake breed name, and it's been hiding in plain sight all episode. 'Leave my fake dog alone' retroactively becomes a much bigger joke. This is Veep writing at its finest — the callback earns everything. Justifies 8+: 'bullshit-tzu' would become the episode's most-quoted line.

jokes.text: last two on the deck of the titanic, huh? / yeah, i think i might jump.
jokes.setup: Two staffers find themselves alone at the party's low point.
jokes.punchline: The Titanic metaphor with Dan's 'I might jump' — a casual suicidal ideation joke delivered as mild preference.
jokes.explanation: Solid dark joke. The Titanic setup is familiar but 'I might jump' as Dan's response is a good character beat. Not exceptional but competent.

jokes.text: you know, i can see right through your shirt. is it designed that way?
jokes.setup: Selina notices someone's sheer shirt at the party.
jokes.punchline: She asks if being see-through is the 'design' — too direct, too specific, in the middle of a political event.
jokes.explanation: Selina's oblivious bluntness is the joke. Functional character comedy but not highly crafted.

jokes.text: oh, what a fistful of fucks.
jokes.setup: The White House requests a 'Sidney Purcell-type figure' for clean jobs.
jokes.punchline: 'A fistful of fucks' — a creative profanity construction replacing 'a fistful of dollars' or similar, expressing maximum frustration.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Selina profanity construction. 'Fistful of fucks' is genuinely original — it's alliterative, it has a physical image, and it's a Clint Eastwood reference applied to political frustration. Justifies high scores: Veep's profanity is always crafted, never lazy, and this is one of the episode's best examples.

jokes.text: the vice president would love to have you on the clean jobs task force, but we both know that would look fucking horrible. / thank you.
jokes.setup: Dan approaches Sidney Purcell with a proposal.
jokes.punchline: He opens by telling Sidney that having him would 'look fucking horrible' — and Sidney thanks him for the compliment.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Washington insider joke. Sidney accepts 'you'd look fucking horrible' as a sincere compliment — because in lobbying, being publicly toxic is a sign of private influence. 'Thank you' as a response to an insult is brilliantly in character for Sidney's world. Justifies 8+: the joke only works if you understand Washington power dynamics, which makes it land with extra force for the target audience.

jokes.text: the vice president would love to have you on the clean jobs task force, but we both know that would look fucking horrible. / thank you.
jokes.setup: Dan approaches Sidney Purcell to explain why he can't be on the task force
jokes.punchline: Sidney thanks Dan for telling him it would look 'fucking horrible' — as if being told your appointment would be a PR disaster is a compliment
jokes.explanation: The joke is Sidney's complete comfort with being told he's a PR disaster — he accepts it as a given and thanks Dan for the directness. This is great Washington satire: everyone knows the game.

jokes.text: oh, really? okay, so you came over to tell me that you're disappointing the shit out of me. i didn't know that. you should have led with that, buddy.
jokes.setup: Dan tells Sidney that Chuck will stay on despite Sidney's opposition.
jokes.punchline: Sidney's mock-surprise at being told disappointing news — 'you should have led with that' — delivered with lobbyist smoothness.
jokes.explanation: Sidney's effortless sarcasm is well-calibrated. 'You should have led with that, buddy' is classic Washington snark. Good character work for a supporting player.

jokes.text: mom, i really hate pictures. / no, honey, you're pretty. / just smile now. smile. / catherine, i'm really serious. smile, okay?
jokes.setup: A photographer wants to take a picture of Selina and Catherine together.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to Catherine's discomfort with photos: 'no, honey, you're pretty' — dismissing the objection as vanity rather than acknowledging Catherine's actual discomfort; then escalating to 'I'm really serious. smile.'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe sequence. Selina hears 'I hate pictures' and processes it as a self-esteem issue to be solved with a compliment ('you're pretty'), completely missing Catherine's point. The escalating commands ('smile... smile... I'm really serious. smile.') are pitch-perfect controlling-mother comedy.

jokes.text: dilka. / dilk-- / what? / dilka. / dilka? / i think her dad's iranian. / iranian?
jokes.setup: Selina asks about Catherine's new roommate.
jokes.punchline: The name 'Dilka' causes repeated double-takes; Selina's processing of 'Iranian' as suspicious/noteworthy is the cringe joke — she's a politician who should know better.
jokes.explanation: Understated political cringe. Selina's alarm at 'Iranian!' is barely masked and completely inappropriate for a senior government official. The staccato exchange is well-timed.

jokes.text: sweet hairy fuck.
jokes.setup: Doyle learns that Sidney Purcell — the most toxic possible oil figure — has ended up on clean jobs.
jokes.punchline: 'Sweet hairy fuck' — Doyle's profane expression of disbelief, which is both crude and oddly poetic.
jokes.explanation: Three words of pure character. 'Sweet hairy fuck' is an original profanity construction — alliterative, physical, and completely unexpected. It's the right size for Doyle's reaction (stunned, not screaming) and it sounds exactly like something a certain type of seasoned senator would say. Justifies 8+: it's highly original and perfectly delivered, and will be quoted for years.

jokes.text: you're changing the name of a hurricane? / yeah. and that would have been a disaster for us. / yeah, naturally. it's amazing. / you're trying to control the weather. you are not fucking thor, mom.
jokes.setup: The team celebrates that they can change the hurricane's name.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's increasingly outraged commentary on her mother's attempted weather control — 'you are not fucking thor, mom.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the hurricane running gag. 'You are not fucking thor, mom' is a teenage outburst that doubles as sharp political commentary — the VP literally trying to control the weather for branding purposes is the show's satire perfectly crystallized. Justifies 8+: it's the episode's thematic statement delivered as a teenager's exasperation, which makes it both funny and resonant.

jokes.text: don't you yes-men ever say no to her? / of course they do. / yeah, we do. yeah. / exactly. / all right, you know what? you're a little bit out of control.
jokes.setup: Catherine calls out the yes-man culture around Selina.
jokes.punchline: Staff immediately say yes to Catherine's question about whether they say no — 'of course they do / yeah, we do' — which is itself a yes-man response. Then Selina calls Catherine 'out of control' for the criticism.
jokes.explanation: Structurally perfect joke. Catherine asks if they're yes-men; they immediately yes-man her question about being yes-men. The self-demonstrating nature of the punchline is exceptional craft. Justifies 8+: this is a joke that would work in a writing seminar as an example of structural elegance.

jokes.text: i've been trying, catherine. i've been trying to talk to you this whole day. / about what? / about everything. i didn't even know that you had a new roommate. what's-his-ass had to tell me. / i know. gary told you.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to express emotional connection with Catherine.
jokes.punchline: 'What's-his-ass' for Gary — Selina literally doesn't know her own aide's name despite spending every day with him, which Catherine immediately knows ('I know. Gary told you.').
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Selina calling Gary 'what's-his-ass' in a scene about emotional connection is darkly funny. Catherine knowing it was Gary and exactly how little her mother pays attention is well-observed.

jokes.text: i've been trying, catherine. i've been trying to talk to you this whole day. / about what? / about everything. / i didn't even know that you had a new roommate. what's-his-ass had to tell me.
jokes.setup: Selina claims she's been trying to connect with Catherine all day
jokes.punchline: 'What's-his-ass had to tell me' — Selina refers to Gary, who told her about the roommate, by 'what's-his-ass'
jokes.explanation: Selina calling Gary 'what's-his-ass' — a person she spends every waking moment with — is a good character beat. It reveals the weirdness of their relationship. The moment is part of a genuine emotional scene, which makes the absurdity land differently.

jokes.text: wait, i'm confused, because now you're begging me to put back onto the task force the same person you were begging me to take off of the task force. / when you say it out loud, you know, it does sound-- / kind of idiotic.
jokes.setup: Doyle returns to ask Selina to reinstate Chuck after previously demanding his removal.
jokes.punchline: Selina crystallizes the absurdity; Doyle half-acknowledges it ('when you say it out loud...'); Selina completes: 'kind of idiotic.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural payoff to the entire Chuck subplot. The episode's political maneuvering is summarized and exposed as absurd in one exchange. 'When you say it out loud' followed by 'kind of idiotic' is beautifully completed. Justifies 8+: it's the political satire thesis of the episode delivered as a conversational punchline.

jokes.text: wait, i'm confused, because now you're begging me to put back onto the task force the same person you were begging me to take off of the task force.
jokes.setup: Senator Doyle has reversed course and now wants Chuck Furnham BACK on the task force
jokes.punchline: Selina calling out the complete reversal with perfect clarity — she's savouring the power reversal
jokes.explanation: Selina's moment of power over Doyle is earned by the episode's Catch-22 structure. Her clinical description of the reversal is both accurate and devastating. The rhythm of 'begging me... same person... begging me' is well-constructed.

jokes.text: well, but it's not as idiotic as the current filibuster system.
jokes.setup: Selina calls Doyle's position 'kind of idiotic.'
jokes.punchline: Doyle's defense: at least it's not as idiotic as the filibuster — a completely unrelated but accurate political observation.
jokes.explanation: Good political joke. The non-sequitur filibuster comparison works because it's both a genuine defense (true, the filibuster is idiotic) and a total deflection. Doyle's confident pivot is very Washington.

jokes.text: catch-44, he caught it. pow! oh, i'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Dan solves the Chuck/Doyle problem simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: Gary celebrates with 'catch-44, he caught it. pow!' — bringing back his earlier 'catch-44' joke. The 'oh, I'm sorry' suggests he physically celebrated too enthusiastically.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Gary's earlier mathematical joke. 'Pow!' followed by an apology for the physical celebration is a nice Gary visual gag.

jokes.text: why wasn't i told about this? / why would you be told about that?
jokes.setup: Jonah asks why he wasn't informed about the dog.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — 'why would you be told about that?' — is the correct and devastating answer. A VP's dog is none of Jonah's business.
jokes.explanation: Good Jonah deflation. His assumption that he should be informed about everything is perfectly skewered. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: you need to kill the dog. / not literally, but-- i mean, yeah, if it comes to it, then literally.
jokes.setup: Jonah reveals the First Lady is also getting a dog, making the VP's dog a PR problem.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's suggestion to 'kill the dog' with an immediate qualifier — 'not literally, but — I mean, yeah, if it comes to it, then literally.'
jokes.explanation: The self-correction that makes things worse is a great comedic structure. 'Not literally' provides a fig leaf that Jonah immediately withdraws. The willingness to literally recommend killing a dog for political optics is peak Jonah.

jokes.text: mike's already got a whole setup for one-- the blankets, the crate, the balls, all the little toys and stuff. / why doesn't he take the pooch?
jokes.setup: They need to get rid of the dog; Dan suggests Mike take it since he already has dog infrastructure
jokes.punchline: Mike has a full dog-care setup despite having no dog — the fake dog running gag paying off practically
jokes.explanation: The fake dog gag paying off: Mike has ALL the equipment for a dog he's never owned. This is strong callback construction — the fake dog has been completely committed to in the physical world, which makes the revelation funnier.

jokes.text: you guys, are we seriously gonna let the guy with the police sketch face of a rapist tell us what to do? / oh, this rapist face gets eights. / consensually, i might throw in.
jokes.setup: Selina objects to following Jonah's dog-killing advice.
jokes.punchline: Staff member rates Jonah's 'rapist face' as getting 'eights' (on a scale of attractiveness presumably), then adds 'consensually' as a qualifier.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist rating of Jonah's 'rapist face' as objectively attractive is very Veep in its willingness to be simultaneously offensive and silly. 'Consensually' as a qualifier is a nice dark touch. The joke is more about office banter than pure craft.

jokes.text: simon is an alpha male.
jokes.setup: Dan suggests Mike take the real dog since he has all the setup
jokes.punchline: Mike defends his fake dog's dominance: 'Simon is an alpha male' — treating a google images photo as a real personality
jokes.explanation: The single funniest Mike line in the episode. Mike defending the personality of his Google Images dog — a fake dog — by calling it an 'alpha male' is perfectly absurd and character-specific. It implies Mike has built a complete fiction around Simon including temperament. Five words, maximum comedy. Economy near-perfect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: seriously, mike has a fake dog. / you're like the only one here who doesn't know that.
jokes.setup: Selina finally learns about the fake dog
jokes.punchline: Catherine and Dan inform Selina she's the last to know — 'you're like the only one here who doesn't know that'
jokes.explanation: The fake dog revelation landing on Selina is a good payoff — she's been the one most invested in dog discourse and was completely fooled. 'You're the only one here who doesn't know' is the devastating confirmation. The running gag lands well.

jokes.text: simon is an alpha male. / actually, i think that is a good idea.
jokes.setup: The suggestion is made that Mike take Catherine's chosen dog since Mike has the setup for a dog.
jokes.punchline: Mike's objection: 'Simon is an alpha male' — defending his fictional dog's status as incompatible with another dog.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. Mike defending his completely imaginary dog's personality and 'alpha' status with total sincerity is brilliant. He's so deep in the lie he's developed a whole personality for Simon. Justifies 8+: the continued commitment to the fiction after exposure is the funniest possible choice.

jokes.text: i just got it from google images. / i'm sorry, ma'am. i thought you knew, ma'am. / otherwise i would not have been laughing. / gary knew? / even fucking gary knew?
jokes.setup: The fake dog revelation plays out — Mike's photo is from Google Images
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating humiliation culminates in 'even fucking gary knew?' — Gary knowing implies the most oblivious person in the room knew before her
jokes.explanation: The capstone of the fake dog storyline. 'Even fucking Gary knew' is the perfect punchline — Gary being aware of something Selina isn't is the ultimate humiliation because Gary is defined by knowing nothing except Selina. The escalation from 'you're the last to know' to specifically Gary knowing is structurally excellent. Extremely quotable and character-specific. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: seriously, mike has a fake dog. you're like the only one here who doesn't know that. / what? / yeah, he uses it to get out of stuff like if he's late. it's called a shitbull terrier. / it's a bullshit-tzu.
jokes.setup: The dog conversation leads to Catherine revealing to Selina that Simon is fake.
jokes.punchline: Dan's version of the fake breed: 'shitbull terrier'; Mike corrects to 'bullshit-tzu' — Mike correcting the name of his own fake dog's fake breed.
jokes.explanation: The culminating payoff of the episode's longest-running joke. Mike correcting the name of his fake dog's fake breed — 'it's a bullshit-tzu, not a shitbull terrier' — is the pinnacle of commitment to the bit. Selina didn't know. Gary knew. The whole office knew except the VP. And Mike still cares enough about the lie's internal consistency to correct Dan on the breed name. This is exceptional structural comedy that earns everything it gets. Near-top score justified.

jokes.text: is this true, mike? / it got a little out of hand. / you carry a picture of it in your wallet, mike. / i just got it from google images. / i'm sorry, ma'am. i thought you knew, ma'am. / otherwise i would not have been laughing. / gary knew? / even fucking gary knew?
jokes.setup: The full reveal of Mike's fake dog Simon to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Each new detail escalates: he has a wallet photo (from Google Images); he didn't tell her; he laughed at her not knowing; even Gary knew — 'even fucking gary knew' as the final indignity.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding escalation sequence. 'I just got it from Google Images' is a devastating detail — he's carrying a stock photo of someone else's dog as his 'pet.' 'Otherwise I would not have been laughing' from the person who was laughing is perfect. 'Even fucking Gary knew' captures Selina's wounded pride at being last to know. Justifies 8+: this is the episode's emotional climax disguised as a comedy beat.

jokes.text: you are getting the dog. you are getting this little rat-faced, repulsive, yappy, crepit-looking terrier.
jokes.setup: Selina, furious about the fake dog revelation and the First Lady blocking, decides she WILL get the dog.
jokes.punchline: She commits to the dog she earlier dismissed in exactly the same terms — 'ball of yappy hair' / 'rat-faced, repulsive, yappy, crepit-looking terrier' — because spite has overcome preference.
jokes.explanation: Perfect full-circle callback. Selina's enthusiasm for a dog she explicitly loathed — using almost identical language ('yappy,' 'rat-faced') from her original rejection — because spite has overridden her preferences. 'Crepit' is presumably 'decrepit' mispronounced in anger. The callback structure across the episode is elegantly resolved. Justifies 8+: it pays off the episode's dog thread and characterizes Selina's spite-driven decision making beautifully.

jokes.text: wow, i'm glad that you were so honest about liking the one that i chose.
jokes.setup: Selina commits to the terrier Gary originally selected, calling it 'rat-faced, repulsive, yappy, crepit.'
jokes.punchline: Gary's mild sarcasm — 'I'm glad you were so honest about liking the one I chose' — is his only moment of barely-concealed hurt.
jokes.explanation: Good Gary callback. His one moment of gentle pushback after being dismissed all episode. The understatement ('I'm glad you were so honest') is very Gary — can't quite bring himself to complain directly.

jokes.text: hey, sue. / jonah. / you got a convenient opening i can slide into? / open up a slot for jonah? / there's not enough alcohol in the world, jonah.
jokes.setup: Jonah makes a transparently sexual double entendre at the party ('a convenient opening I can slide into').
jokes.punchline: Sue's response: 'there's not enough alcohol in the world, jonah' — the most efficient possible rejection.
jokes.explanation: Sue's absolute refusal to engage on Jonah's terms, expressed with maximum contempt ('not enough alcohol in the world'), is a great character moment. The economy is near-perfect. A great Jonah/Sue dynamic beat.

jokes.text: Hey, Sue. / Jonah. / You got a convenient opening I can slide into?
jokes.setup: Jonah approaches Sue at the party
jokes.punchline: 'Convenient opening I can slide into' — an unambiguously sexual double entendre deployed as a professional question, apparently genuinely intended as either/both
jokes.explanation: The opener is scored separately from the three-beat sequence (joke 83) because it functions as its own punchline. The double entendre is so brazen it may be Jonah's actual intent. 'Open up a slot for Jonah?' as the follow-up makes it explicit. Classic Jonah — the worst possible social strategy executed with complete confidence.

jokes.text: didn't say no. / no!
jokes.setup: Sue says 'there's not enough alcohol' — which technically isn't a 'no.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah seizes on the technicality ('didn't say no'); Sue issues an explicit 'no!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the Sue/Jonah exchange. Jonah's lawyer-brained exploitation of 'didn't say no' is exactly his character, and Sue's blunt 'no!' closes the loop. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: let's go mingle the shit out of 'em.
jokes.setup: Selina and Catherine prepare to mingle at the party.
jokes.punchline: 'Mingle the shit out of 'em' — applying aggressive, combative language to the gentle social act of mingling.
jokes.explanation: Good Selina line. The militaristic framing of party socializing ('mingle the shit out of 'em') is very her. Economical and quotable.

jokes.text: ernest lainchbury, lifeboat association. / he's got a glass eye. / i am so happy you're here.
jokes.setup: Gary is feeding Selina names and details of guests as she mingles.
jokes.punchline: Gary's note: 'he's got a glass eye.' Selina responds with 'I am so happy you're here' — a response to the glass eye warning that is completely bizarre and slightly manic.
jokes.explanation: The glass eye warning and Selina's deflective enthusiasm response is a good bit of physical comedy implied through dialogue. Why Selina is so happy someone with a glass eye is there is funnier left unexplained.

jokes.text: jenny armitage, disabled sports of america. / just had triplets. / oh, triplets. wow. / that must have hurt... unless you had a c-section.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces a guest who works in disabled sports and just had triplets.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'that must have hurt... unless you had a c-section' — an unnecessary physical speculation about someone's childbirth method at a party.
jokes.explanation: Selina's compulsive oversharing of physical speculation is good character comedy. The 'unless you had a c-section' qualifier makes it worse, not better — exactly the kind of thing she can't stop herself from saying.

jokes.text: carlos esquerra, c.e.o. of the nasdaq o.m.x. group. / i got nothing. use your spanish. / su casa es mi casa. / mi casa. / mi casa es su casa.
jokes.setup: Gary has no intel on a guest, so suggests Selina 'use your Spanish.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's Spanish: 'su casa es mi casa' — she gets the phrase backwards (it should be 'mi casa es su casa'), which Gary corrects. She then completes the corrected version, having half-absorbed the note.
jokes.explanation: Great bit. Selina deploying her entire Spanish vocabulary (one idiom, backwards) is funny; Gary's correction during the live interaction is excellent; Selina then completing the corrected version as if she's been corrected mid-performance is perfectly observed. Well-constructed three-beat joke.

jokes.text: rt van der merkle, head of gambling commission. / pro-gambling. loves to kill. / hey, there.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces a guest with minimal intel.
jokes.punchline: 'Pro-gambling. Loves to kill.' — delivered as if these are normal party guest descriptors. Selina's 'hey, there' is the only possible response.
jokes.explanation: Wonderful Gary moment. 'Pro-gambling. Loves to kill.' delivered as briefing intel is deadpan perfection. The economy is exceptional — three words that create a complete character portrait that Selina must then navigate with only 'hey, there.' Justifies 8+: this is the Gary-oke system at its funniest.

jokes.text: corey wilk, special advisor to the supreme court of michigan. / he's got a brother in rage against the machine. / tell your brother i love his music.
jokes.setup: Gary's intel on Corey Wilk is: he has a brother in Rage Against the Machine.
jokes.punchline: Selina, talking to the Michigan Supreme Court advisor, immediately brings up his brother in Rage Against the Machine — a surreal non-sequitur that makes the interaction deeply awkward.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist leap from 'Supreme Court of Michigan' to 'Rage Against the Machine' is funny because of how unconnected they are. Selina using the brother info as an icebreaker with the actual person is wonderfully awkward.

jokes.text: carrie stringer, center for social action. / he's a triathlete. / no, he's not.
jokes.setup: Gary gives Selina a detail about a guest — 'he's a triathlete.'
jokes.punchline: Selina: 'no, he's not' — she can see Carrie Stringer and immediately contradicts Gary's intel.
jokes.explanation: Three words that imply Gary's research is wrong and Carrie Stringer looks decidedly un-athletic. The gap between Gary's briefing and physical reality is the joke. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: liam miller, nasa. / okay, that's an acronym for national aeronautics-- / i-- stop it. / okay.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces someone from NASA and begins to spell out the acronym.
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts him off — 'I-- stop it' — the idea that she might not know what NASA stands for, and Gary's impulse to explain it to the Vice President, is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Gary's automated 'that's an acronym for...' with Selina's mortified stop is a perfect beat. The implication that Gary has briefed enough confused people that he automatically explains NASA is wonderful. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: the reverend terrance clark, church of the living christ. / he's a baker. / oh, christ has risen.
jokes.setup: Gary introduces a reverend and notes he's a baker.
jokes.punchline: Selina: 'oh, christ has risen' — a double pun combining the religious ('christ is risen' — Easter) with the baking ('risen' bread). And it's an enthusiastic, genuine-sounding response.
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun. 'Christ has risen' works on two levels simultaneously (baking/religious) and Selina's delivery — as if it's genuine enthusiasm — makes it land as either brilliant improv or unintentional genius. The economy is near-perfect: three words, two meanings, one perfect set-up. Justifies 8+: it's the kind of double-meaning that earns both a groan and genuine admiration.

jokes.text: hyun-joo lee, urban placemakers forum. / he's korean.
jokes.setup: Gary is providing intel on guests to help Selina schmooze.
jokes.punchline: For Hyun-joo Lee, Gary's entire intel contribution is: 'he's Korean.' The implication that Gary has nothing else and defaults to ethnicity is cringe-funny.
jokes.explanation: Good closing beat for the Gary-oke sequence. The contrast between his previous detailed briefings and this single ethnic descriptor is the joke. It's funny because Gary means well and has absolutely nothing to offer beyond this one observation.

jokes.text: hyun-joo lee, urban placemakers forum. / he's korean.
jokes.setup: Gary is feeding Selina information on guests so she can make small talk
jokes.punchline: For Hyun-joo Lee, Gary's only intel is 'he's Korean' — as if this is useful networking information
jokes.explanation: The punchline of the entire mingle sequence. After a stream of surprisingly specific-but-useless facts, Gary's offering for the last guest is just their ethnicity — the most blithely useless and tone-deaf item yet. The sequence structure (increasingly odd info) makes this land as a strong closer. Economy is near-perfect.

tmdb_overview: Selina celebrates 20 years in Washington, D.C., but tries to keep a low profile after a newspaper article paints her as a diva and rumors float about her feud with the first lady. Meanwhile, Selina’s college-age daughter arrives for a visit, and is surprised that her mother is looking for a dog but appalled by her reaction to “Selina” being on the list of names for hurricanes.
insight: Early season finds its rhythm: 78 jokes in 39 minutes, cringe-led without standouts.
